stories.title: Track EP Category Internship Obligation per 1:3 Internship Policy (TalentCorp/MOHR)
stories.description: Malaysia's Ministry of Human Resources (MOHR) and TalentCorp mandated that companies employing foreign nationals via Employment Pass (EP) must offer structured paid internships to Malaysian students: 3 placements per EP Cat I, 2 per Cat II, 1 per Cat III. Full enforcement begins 1 January 2026 (pilot extended to March 2026). Payroll must track EP category per expatriate, calculate the total internship quota, and record fulfilled placements (minimum 10 weeks, RM500–RM600/month stipend) to produce the annual evidence package supporting future EP renewal applications.
acceptanceCriteria: Employee record stores Employment Pass category (Cat I / Cat II / Cat III) with EP approval reference number and approval date
Company dashboard calculates total internship slots required = sum(EP Cat I × 3 + EP Cat II × 2 + EP Cat III × 1), capped at 2% of total employee headcount
Intern Placement doctype records: intern name, qualification level, TalentCorp programme reference, start/end date, stipend amount, fulfilment status
System validates intern stipend meets minimum: RM600/month for degree/master/DLKM level, RM500/month for diploma/SKM/certificate level
Compliance summary report shows total quota vs. fulfilled placements, with gap count highlighted in red when unfulfilled quota > 0
Alert triggered 60 days before EP renewal date if internship quota not fully met for the current 12-month period
technicalNotes: Add `employment_pass_category` (Select: Cat I / Cat II / Cat III) and `ep_approval_reference` fields to Employee fixture
New DocType `Internship Placement` linked to Company with fields: intern_name, qualification_level, talentcorp_reference, start_date, end_date, monthly_stipend, status
Dashboard card on LHDN Payroll workspace showing quota compliance ratio
Pilot phase extended to 31 March 2026; mandatory from 1 January 2026
Non-compliance does not cancel existing EPs but adversely impacts future EP renewal assessment

stories.title: PDPA 2024 DPO Appointment Registry and 72-Hour Payroll Data Breach Notification Workflow
stories.description: The Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Act 2024, effective 1 June 2025, requires employers processing large-scale employee payroll data to (a) appoint a Data Protection Officer (DPO), (b) notify the Commissioner of the DPO appointment within 21 days, (c) upon detecting a payroll data breach, notify the Commissioner within 72 hours and affected employees within 7 days. These are materially different obligations from general PDPA compliance — the existing module covers data portability and access rights, but not the DPO appointment record-keeping or breach incident management workflows mandated by the February 2025 Commissioner Guidelines.
acceptanceCriteria: Company record stores DPO name, contact email, phone number, appointment date, and Commissioner registration date
System alerts HR Admin if 21-day DPO Commissioner notification deadline not recorded after DPO appointment date

New 'Payroll Data Breach Incident' DocType with fields: discovery_datetime, breach_type, affected_records_count, data_categories_affected, risk_assessment, commissioner_notified_datetime, employee_notification_datetime
72-hour countdown alert activates upon incident creation; red critical alert displayed if commissioner_notified_datetime is blank and 72 hours have elapsed since discovery_datetime
System generates a pre-filled Commissioner Notification Letter (PDF) with breach details, number of affected employees, and risk assessment summary
System generates pre-filled Employee Breach Notification email template once Commissioner has been notified (7-day deadline from Commissioner notification)
Audit log records all breach incident updates with user and timestamp
technicalNotes: New DocType `PDPA Breach Incident` linked to Company; status flow: Draft → Commissioner Notified → Employees Notified → Resolved
DPO fields on Company doctype in `PDPA Compliance` tab via custom field fixture
Commissioner notification required within 72 hours of breach awareness (not breach occurrence) per Guideline on Data Breach Notification (Feb 2025)
Employee notification required within 7 days of Commissioner notification — only required if breach poses significant harm to data subjects
Guideline published: https://www.pdp.gov.my/ppdpv1/en/ — Guideline on Data Breach Notification and DPO Appointment

stories.title: MySIP Structured Internship Stipend Minimum Threshold Validation and Double Tax Deduction Payroll Tagging
stories.description: Employers participating in TalentCorp's National Structured Internship Programme (MySIP) are entitled to a double corporate tax deduction on internship-related expenses (stipends, training costs) from YA 2017 to YA 2025, with potential extension. Payroll must identify intern employees, validate their monthly stipend meets the MySIP minimum threshold, track the TalentCorp Endorsement Letter reference, and produce a year-end summary of MySIP-qualifying expenses per intern for the corporate tax claim. This is a corporate tax benefit tracking feature, not a statutory payroll deduction.
acceptanceCriteria: Employee record has flag `is_mysip_intern` (Check) and fields: `mysip_qualification_level` (Select: Master/Degree/DLKM | Diploma/SKM/Certificate), `talentcorp_endorsement_reference` (Data)
On payroll run, if `is_mysip_intern` = true, system validates stipend >= RM600 for degree-level and >= RM500 for diploma-level interns; warning displayed if below threshold
Year-end MySIP Summary Report shows: intern name, qualification level, endorsement reference, total stipend paid, total qualifying expenses for double deduction claim
Report includes total claimable double deduction amount = 2x (total stipend + training costs)
Each qualifying Salary Slip is tagged with `mysip_eligible: true` for audit trail
technicalNotes: Minimum stipends: RM600/month for Master, Degree, DLKM, Professional Certificate; RM500/month for Diploma, DKM, SKM Level 1-3
Double deduction applies to: intern allowances, training materials, accommodation/transport
TalentCorp issues Endorsement Letter based on annual internship placements submitted
MySIP double deduction covered YA2017-YA2025; check Budget 2026 for extension status
Reference: https://www.talentcorp.com.my/mysip-incentives/

stories.title: HRD Corp SBL-KHAS Grant Pre-Approval Compliance and 6-Month Post-Training Claim Deadline Alert
stories.description: HRD Corp requires employers to submit a training grant application via eTRiS at least 1 working day before training begins — applications submitted after training starts are automatically rejected. After training completes, claims must be submitted within 30 days (preferred) and no later than 6 months (hard deadline). The existing 'HRD Corp eTRIS Training Claim Recording and Levy Utilization Dashboard' story records claims already submitted; this story covers the pre-approval compliance workflow and deadline alert system to prevent rejected or expired claims.
acceptanceCriteria: Training Event doctype has fields: training_start_date, training_end_date, etris_application_date, etris_approval_number, etris_claim_submission_date, grant_amount_approved, grant_amount_claimed
System alerts HR Manager if training_start_date is reached and etris_application_date is blank (pre-approval not submitted)
System flags training as non-HRD-claimable if etris_application_date >= training_start_date (post-training application — auto-rejected by HRD Corp)
Alert at 20 days and 28 days after training_end_date if etris_claim_submission_date is blank (30-day deadline approaching)
Critical alert escalated to HR Director at 5 months and 5.5 months after training_end_date if claim still not submitted (6-month absolute deadline)
Dashboard shows: trainings needing pre-approval, expiring claim windows (within 30 days), expired claim windows (failed to claim), and rejected grants with reason codes
technicalNotes: HRD Corp eTRiS portal: hrdcorp.gov.my
Grant approval takes 10-14 working days — apply well before training start
SBL-KHAS is the main grant scheme requiring application at least 1 day before training
6-month claim deadline is absolute — late submissions cannot be appealed or recovered
This extends the existing levy utilization dashboard by adding pre-training compliance tracking

stories.title: Employment Act S.60F(2): Track 60-Day Hospitalization Leave Quota Separately from Ordinary Sick Leave
stories.description: The Employment Act 1955 S.60F provides two distinct sick leave entitlements: (1) ordinary paid sick leave of 14-22 days based on service years, and (2) additional hospital/in-patient leave of up to 60 days per year when an employee is hospitalized or requires leave following hospitalization as certified by a registered medical practitioner. These must be tracked separately — hospitalization leave does not reduce ordinary sick leave entitlement. The existing 'Track Employment Act S.60F Sick Leave Entitlement per Service Band' story covers ordinary sick leave; this story addresses the distinct 60-day hospitalization leave quota.
acceptanceCriteria: Two distinct Leave Types: 'Sick Leave (EA)' for ordinary sick leave (14-22 days by service band) and 'Hospitalization Leave (EA)' for in-patient/post-hospitalization (60 days annual)
Hospitalization Leave allocation of 60 days auto-created for all EA-covered employees at each leave year start
Hospitalization Leave application requires: `hospitalization_discharge_date` (Date) and `medical_certificate_type` (Select: Hospitalization Certificate | Post-Hospitalization Medical Advice)
Leave balance summary on payslip shows both balances separately: 'Sick Leave (EA): X days' and 'Hospitalization Leave (EA): Y days'
System validates Hospitalization Leave applications are processed against the 60-day quota, not the ordinary sick leave quota
Alert to HR if employee Hospitalization Leave balance reaches zero — no further hospitalization leave available for the year
technicalNotes: EA S.60F(2): Up to 60 days paid hospitalization leave when certified by registered medical practitioner that in-patient care is necessary
EA S.60F(1): Ordinary sick leave: 14 days (<2 years service), 18 days (2-5 years), 22 days (>5 years)
Both entitlements paid at ordinary rate of pay
Employee may use both entitlements in same year — they are separate pools
Reference: Employment Act 1955 S.60F — https://jtksm.mohr.gov.my/sites/default/files/2023-11/Akta%20Kerja%201955%20(Akta%20265).pdf

stories.title: Employment Act S.60A: Annual Leave Carry-Forward Cap and Cash-Out Enforcement at Year-End
stories.description: Under Employment Act 1955 S.60A, unused annual leave that is not taken within the leave year must either be carried forward (by agreement) or paid out at the basic rate. The EA 2022 Amendment strengthened employee protections — employers cannot unilaterally forfeit unused leave without the employee's agreement. ERPNext's standard leave module does not enforce Malaysian EA-specific carry-forward rules or the mandatory cash-out offer before forfeiture. Payroll must enforce that expiring leave is either carried forward or paid out, with documented employee consent for any forfeiture.
acceptanceCriteria: Annual Leave (EA) carry-forward is configurable with maximum carry-forward days (default: 1x annual entitlement, e.g., max 22 days)
At leave year-end, system generates 'Leave Expiry Notice' per employee showing: days expiring, cash-out amount at basic daily rate, carry-forward option
HR Manager must record employee's decision (Carry Forward | Cash Out | Mutual Forfeiture Agreement) before closing leave year for that employee
Cash-out of unused annual leave = (Monthly Basic Salary / 26) × leave days, recorded as non-statutory pay component on Salary Slip
System blocks leave year close-off for any employee without a recorded leave expiry decision
Audit log records all leave carry-forward and cash-out decisions with HR manager approval and employee acknowledgement timestamp
technicalNotes: EA S.60A(3): Employer must pay employee for unused annual leave on termination at basic rate
EA S.60A(4): Leave should be utilized within 12 months of entitlement year
Cash-out rate: Basic Rate of Pay per day = Monthly Basic Salary / 26 (or number of working days per month per contract)
EA 2022 Amendment added protections against unilateral forfeiture
Reference: Employment Act 1955 S.60A

stories.title: FWCMS Foreign Worker Annual Levy Renewal Expiry Date Tracking and Advance Alert per Employee
stories.description: Foreign worker levies in Malaysia are paid annually via the FWCMS portal, with the expiry date tied to each individual worker's PLKS/permit period (not a fixed calendar date). The existing 'FWCMS Foreign Worker Levy: 10th-of-Month Payment Deadline Alert' story covers monthly payment monitoring; this story covers the annual renewal cycle per individual worker. Employers with many foreign workers at different renewal dates face compliance risk if individual levy expiry dates are not tracked — an expired levy can result in immediate non-compliance and worker removal orders.
acceptanceCriteria: Employee record (foreign worker) stores: `fwcms_levy_payment_date`, `fwcms_levy_expiry_date`, `fwcms_receipt_reference`, `fwcms_levy_sector` (Select: Manufacturing | Construction | Plantation | Agriculture | Services)
Levy sector rate table: Manufacturing/Construction/Services = RM1,850/year; Plantation/Agriculture = RM640/year (configurable for future rate changes)
Alerts sent to HR Admin at 90, 60, and 30 days before each worker's individual levy expiry date
Critical alert escalated at 14 days before expiry if renewal receipt not yet recorded
Non-compliant workers dashboard widget: shows workers with levy expiring in <30 days, <60 days, and already expired
Upon recording renewal, system validates receipt reference is provided and auto-calculates next expiry = payment date + 365 days (or per PLKS duration if different)
technicalNotes: FWCMS levy is annual, not monthly — renewal date is per worker based on PLKS expiry
Manufacturing, Construction, Services: RM1,850/year per worker
Plantation, Agriculture: RM640/year per worker
Multi-tier levy increase of RM300-500 under deliberation — rate table must be configurable
FWCMS portal: https://www.fwcms.gov.my — levy paid online, employer receives electronic receipt
Levy paid by employer — cannot deduct from employee salary

stories.title: PCB 2026 Specification: Enforce Truncation-then-5-Cent-Ceiling Rounding Rule in MTD Calculation Engine
stories.description: The official LHDN PCB 2026 Computerised Calculation Specification mandates a specific two-step rounding rule that is different from standard mathematical rounding: Step 1 — truncate the raw PCB amount to exactly 2 decimal places (dropping subsequent digits without rounding); Step 2 — apply ceiling rounding up to the nearest 5 cents. For example: 123.023 → truncated to 123.02 → rounded to 123.05; and 123.061 → truncated to 123.06 → rounded to 123.10. If the current MTD engine uses standard banker's rounding or simple 2dp rounding, it will produce incorrect PCB values that diverge from LHDN's expected amounts, potentially causing CP39 submission mismatches.
acceptanceCriteria: MTD calculation engine applies two-step rounding as final step: truncated = floor(raw_pcb * 100) / 100; final_pcb = ceil(truncated / 0.05) * 0.05
All PCB calculation paths (Method 1 regular salary and Method 2 YTD recalculation) pass through the same rounding function
CP39 CSV output values are in 2dp format matching the 5-cent rounded amounts
Dedicated unit test class `TestPCB2026Rounding` covers at least 15 representative scenarios including edge cases at 0.00, 0.01, 0.05, 0.06 fractional values
Rounding function is extracted into a shared utility `pcb_utils.round_pcb(amount)` callable from all calculation paths
technicalNotes: From LHDN PCB 2026 Spec: 'Pengiraan berkomputer PCB hendaklah dalam dua titik perpuluhan dan mesti mengabaikan sebarang angka berikutnya (cth: 123.023 ≈ 123.02). Dari situ, amaun perlu dibundarkan kepada 5 sen yang paling hampir ke atas; cth: 123.02 ≈ 123.05 manakala 123.06 ≈ 123.10'
Use Python Decimal module to avoid floating-point precision errors: from decimal import Decimal, ROUND_DOWN
Step 1: truncated = Decimal(str(raw)).quantize(Decimal('0.01'), rounding=ROUND_DOWN)
Step 2: import math; final = math.ceil(float(truncated) / 0.05) * 0.05; final = Decimal(str(final)).quantize(Decimal('0.01'))
Reference: https://www.hasil.gov.my/media/arvlrzh5/spesifikasi-kaedah-pengiraan-berkomputer-pcb-2026.pdf

stories.title: PDPA 2024: Data Processor Agreement Compliance Checklist for External Payroll Service Vendors
stories.description: The Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Act 2024, effective June 2025, introduces direct legal obligations on data processors — including third-party payroll bureaus, statutory submission agents, and HRMS vendors who process employee salary, PCB, EPF, and statutory data. Employers using external payroll services must ensure a valid Data Processing Agreement (DPA) is in place with each processor. This is distinct from the existing DPO appointment and breach notification stories — it specifically addresses the contractual compliance layer between the employer (data controller) and any external party handling payroll data.
acceptanceCriteria: New 'Data Processor Registry' section on Company record listing all third-party data processors with: processor_name, services_provided, dpa_signed_date, dpa_expiry_date, dpa_document_attachment, last_security_audit_date
Alert to HR/Legal when any DPA expires within 30 days or has expired
Export of employee personal data (salary, IC number, TIN, bank account) triggers an audit log entry showing: user, data category, downstream processor, timestamp
Compliance checklist report confirms: DPA on file (Yes/No), DPA current (Yes/No), DPO notified of processor (Yes/No) per each registered processor
System warns if bulk payroll data export is attempted without any active DPA on file for the receiving processor
technicalNotes: PDPA 2024 S.XX: Data processors now have direct liability for security principle breaches — fine up to RM1,000,000 or 3 years imprisonment
DPA must specify: purpose of processing, data categories, retention period, security measures, data subject rights handling
Effective date of data processor direct obligations: 1 June 2025
Processors typically include: HRD Corp submission agents, LHDN e-PCB Plus submission bureaus, bank payroll disbursement services, EPF/SOCSO e-filing vendors
Reference: https://www.pdp.gov.my/ppdpv1/wp-content/uploads/2024/11/Act-A1727.pdf

stories.title: Budget 2026 Expanded Double Deduction for Training Disabled Care Workers (YA2026-2027) — Payroll Evidence Package
stories.description: Malaysia Budget 2026 expanded the existing double tax deduction for sponsoring OKU training to also cover employers who sponsor training costs for care workers who support OKU (Orang Kurang Upaya) individuals, effective YA2026 and YA2027 only. This is a new category distinct from the existing OKU employee double deduction and the general HRD Corp levy claims. Payroll must distinguish care worker training from regular training, apply the YA2026-2027 date gate, and generate the annual evidence schedule for corporate tax submission.
acceptanceCriteria: Training Event doctype has `deduction_category` field (Select: Standard | HRD Corp | OKU Employee Training | OKU Care Worker Training [Budget 2026])
When `deduction_category` = 'OKU Care Worker Training', system flags the record as qualifying for Budget 2026 double deduction
Date gate: OKU Care Worker Training double deduction only valid for training completed between 1 January 2026 and 31 December 2027; system blocks this category for training outside this range
Year-end Summary Report shows separate line: 'Budget 2026 OKU Care Worker Training Double Deduction — Qualifying Expenses: RM X, Claimable Deduction: RM 2X'
Alert if 'OKU Care Worker Training' is selected for training outside YA2026-2027 range
technicalNotes: From Budget 2026: 'The double deduction for sponsoring training for persons with disabilities is expanded to include sponsoring training for care workers for YA2026-YA2027'
Care worker = workers providing direct care/support services to OKU individuals (not the OKU employee themselves)
Qualifying expenses: course fees, training materials, trainer fees — same rules as standard OKU training deduction
This is a corporate income tax deduction claimed in the company's annual return — not a payroll withholding deduction

Source: Crowe Malaysia Budget 2026 Highlights — https://www.crowe.com/my/-/media/crowe/firms/asia-pacific/my/crowemy/news/key-highlights-of-malaysias-budget-2026--for-individuals-and-businesses-newsletter.pdf

stories.title: Enforce e-CP22 Online-Only Mandatory Submission and MyTax Employer Representative Authorization Check
stories.description: From 1 September 2024, LHDN mandated that all CP22 new employee notifications must be submitted exclusively through the e-CP22 system on MyTax Portal — manual paper submissions to LHDN offices are no longer accepted. Critically, the submission can only be made by the company director (with MyTax Employer role) or a registered Employer Representative (Wakil Majikan). The existing 'Auto-Submit CP22 via e-CP22 MyTax Within 30 Days' story focuses on the 30-day deadline tracking; this story enforces the online-only mandate, validates Employer Representative setup, and blocks paper CP22 generation.
acceptanceCriteria: CP22 data generation produces only a digital summary for manual MyTax upload — no PDF formatted for paper/postal submission to LHDN office
Company record stores MyTax Employer Representative (Wakil Majikan): name, MyTax login ID, authorization date — required field before e-CP22 workflow is accessible
Alert displayed: 'e-CP22 must be submitted by company director or registered Wakil Majikan via mytax.hasil.gov.my — system cannot auto-submit'
After manual submission, HR records LHDN acknowledgement reference number on the Employee record (this reference is visible in MyTax after submission)
System blocks new employee onboarding finalization if CP22 status = 'Pending' and 30 days have elapsed since hire date
System shows penalty notice when 30-day deadline at risk: 'Offence under ITA 1967 S.83(2) — Fine RM200 to RM20,000 or 6 months imprisonment or both'
technicalNotes: Mandatory online-only e-CP22 submission effective: 1 September 2024
Authorized submitters: Company Director (MyTax Employer role) OR registered Wakil Majikan — not HR staff without MyTax authorization
LHDN issues acknowledgement reference upon successful e-CP22 submission — must be stored on Employee record for audit
Manual CP22 paper form no longer valid after September 2024 — LHDN offices will not accept them
Reference: https://landco.my/social/cp22-online/ and https://www.hasil.gov.my/en/employers/notifications-of-new-employee/

stories.title: TP1(1/2026): Capture New Domestic Tourism Attraction Expenses Relief (RM1,000) in Employee PCB Declaration
stories.description: Malaysia Budget 2026 introduced a brand-new individual income tax relief category in the TP1(1/2026) form: expenses for entrance fees to local tourist attractions and art/cultural programmes conducted within Malaysia, capped at RM1,000 per year of assessment. This category has never appeared in any prior TP1 form. The PCB engine must add this as a new deductible item when employees declare it on their TP1(1/2026) relief form. While the generic 'Update TP1(1/2026) Relief Categories' story addresses the budget's general TP1 changes, domestic tourism is an entirely new relief code with no prior-year equivalent requiring discrete field mapping, validation, and PCB computation integration.
acceptanceCriteria: TP1 Declaration form adds field: `domestic_tourism_expenses` (Currency, label: 'Entrance fees to local tourist attractions / art & cultural programmes in Malaysia')
PCB calculation deducts declared `domestic_tourism_expenses` (capped at RM1,000) from chargeable income when computing monthly MTD
Field only available in TP1(1/2026) form — not retroactively applied to prior-year TP1 records
Validation: amount cannot exceed RM1,000; system shows 'Maximum RM1,000 for YA2026 domestic tourism relief' if exceeded
Year-end PCB reconciliation report shows domestic tourism relief declared per employee
This field is absent from TP3(1/2026) — domestic tourism is a current-employer TP1 item, not a prior-employer YTD income item
technicalNotes: From LHDN TP1(1/2026) explanatory notes: new relief for 'Entrance fees to local tourist attractions and art/cultural programmes within Malaysia'
Maximum: RM1,000 per year of assessment (YA2026 only — no retroactive application)
Source form: https://www.hasil.gov.my/media/gjuhcbc2/bi-tp1-form-2026.pdf
Explanatory notes: https://www.hasil.gov.my/media/i1lcd0wn/bi-explanatory-notes-for-from-tp1-2026.pdf
Confirmed also by briohr.com YA2026 update: 'New Reliefs: Domestic Tourism Expenses — Entrance fees to local tourist attractions — Relief cap: RM1,000'
